title: Revamp quality profiles for radarr/sonarr
description:
Quality profiles in recyclarr are outdated and too restrictive. Need to:

## Problems
1. **Stale profiles**: Current custom format scores and quality profiles haven't been updated in a long time — TRaSH Guides have likely evolved significantly
2. **Tool evaluation**: Investigate configarr as a potential replacement or complement to recyclarr. Configarr may offer more flexibility/features.
3. **Missing lenient profiles**: Min score of 1000 on all profiles prevents automatic fetching for older/obscure content that users request. Need tiered profiles (e.g., a 'Fallback' or 'Lenient' profile with a lower/zero minimum score for content that won't get top-quality grabs)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Evaluate configarr vs recyclarr — decide which to use going forward
- Update custom format definitions to current TRaSH Guides recommendations
- Add at minimum one lower-threshold quality profile for older/obscure content (min score 0 or low)
- Radarr and Sonarr both covered
- Automated sync still working after changes

title: Platform Maintenance & Tooling
description: Ongoing platform maintenance and tooling improvements: PostgreSQL major version upgrade, evaluate and adopt Tuppr for Talos upgrades, evaluate Volsync perfectra1n fork, evaluate Taskfile-to-Justfile migration, deploy Actions Runner Controller for self-hosted CI, deploy Notifier webhook service, integrate Minecraft world backups, and complete Open WebUI migration.

title: Smart Home / IoT Stack
description: Deploy the full IoT automation stack on the cluster: MQTT broker, Zigbee2MQTT for Zigbee device management, and ESPHome for ESP-based device firmware.

title: Authentication & SSO
description: Deploy Authentik as the cluster-wide identity provider and configure OIDC/SSO across all cluster services. Integrate with Tailscale identity headers and use auth proxies for services without native OIDC support.

title: Networking & Ingress Modernization
description: Modernize cluster networking: migrate deprecated Cilium BGP CRDs to v2, migrate all Ingress resources to Gateway API HTTPRoutes, set up internal TLS with TinyCA, and integrate the Tailscale Kubernetes Operator for native tailnet management.

title: Set up comprehensive OIDC auth across cluster services
description:
Implement OIDC-based authentication integrated with Tailscale identity headers. Configure SSO for backend services that support it (Grafana, BookLore, Overseerr, Open WebUI, etc). Evaluate auth proxy options (e.g. oauth2-proxy, Authelia) for services without native OIDC. Leverage Tailscale's HTTP_TAILSCALE_USER_LOGIN headers already used by Paperless as a pattern.

## Acceptance Criteria
- OIDC provider deployed and configured
- Tailscale identity headers integrated with auth flow
- Grafana, BookLore, Overseerr, and Open WebUI configured for SSO
- Services without native OIDC use auth proxy
- Documentation of which services use native OIDC vs proxy
notes: pocket-id (OIDC provider) and tinyauth (ForwardAuth proxy) deployed to auth namespace. External gateway + cloudflared-auth tunnel wired up. Remaining: configure individual service OIDC (Grafana, BookLore, Open WebUI, etc.) and ForwardAuth annotations per-app.

title: Integrate Minecraft world backups via itzg/docker-mc-backup
description:
Add itzg/docker-mc-backup for Minecraft world backups to restic/B2. Ensure this doesn't duplicate existing Volsync PVC backups - likely need to disable Volsync for Minecraft PVCs and use mc-backup instead since it handles world save-off/save-on for consistency, which Volsync snapshots can't guarantee. Reference: https://github.com/itzg/docker-mc-backup

## Acceptance Criteria
- mc-backup sidecar deployed alongside Minecraft server pods
- Backups stored in B2 via restic
- Volsync disabled for Minecraft PVCs to avoid duplication
- Backup schedule configured and verified with successful restore test

title: Automate book downloads to BookLore drop folder
description:
Set up automation to automatically move downloaded books into BookLore's book drop/import folder. Investigate integration points - download client watch folder, shared NFS mount, or sidecar that monitors a download directory and moves files to BookLore's consumption path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Downloaded books automatically appear in BookLore's drop folder
- No manual file moving required
- Works with existing download workflow
close_reason: Implemented: shared NFS bookdrop at /volume1/media/bookdrop, BookLore mounts it at /bookdrop, OpenBooks writes there directly, qbt post-process.sh copies category=books torrents there, qbtools excludes site:mam from both prune jobs

title: Complete Open WebUI migration

## Acceptance Criteria

- Ollama models accessible and responding
- User accounts configured
- CORS properly restricted to allowed origins

title: Advanced Cilium tuning (BBR, Maglev, netkit eBPF)
description:
Apply advanced Cilium configuration from reference repos for improved networking performance.

## Current Setup (Your Repo)

```yaml
# kubernetes/apps/kube-system/cilium/app/helmrelease.yaml
# Minimal config - relies on ConfigMap for main values
values:
  hubble:
    enabled: true
    # ... hubble config
  operator:
    prometheus:
      enabled: true
  prometheus:
    enabled: true
```

**Missing advanced features** that both reference repos have enabled.

## Reference Setup (buroa & onedr0p)

Both repos have extensive Cilium tuning:

```yaml
# Key performance settings
kubeProxyReplacement: true          # Replace kube-proxy entirely
routingMode: native                 # Native routing vs overlay
bpf:
  datapathMode: netkit              # Kernel-space eBPF datapath
  masquerade: true
bandwidthManager:
  enabled: true
  bbr: true                         # BBR congestion control
ipv4:
  enabled: true
enableIPv4BIGTCP: true              # Big TCP for high throughput
loadBalancer:
  algorithm: maglev                 # Consistent hashing LB
  mode: dsr                         # Direct Server Return
  acceleration: native              # Hardware acceleration
bgpControlPlane:
  enabled: true                     # Native BGP for routing
```

## Key Configuration Differences

| Feature | Your Setup | Reference | Impact |
|---------|------------|-----------|--------|
| kubeProxyReplacement | Unknown | `true` | Removes kube-proxy overhead |
| bpf.datapathMode | Default | `netkit` | Kernel-space processing |
| bandwidthManager.bbr | Disabled | `true` | Better congestion control |
| enableIPv4BIGTCP | Disabled | `true` | Higher throughput |
| loadBalancer.algorithm | Default | `maglev` | Consistent hashing |
| loadBalancer.mode | Default | `dsr` | Direct Server Return |
| bgpControlPlane | Unknown | `true` | Dynamic BGP routing |

## Why Tune?

1. **kubeProxyReplacement**: Eliminates iptables overhead, pure eBPF
2. **netkit datapath**: Processes packets in kernel space, lower latency
3. **BBR bandwidth manager**: Google's congestion control, better throughput
4. **BIGTCP**: Larger TCP segments for high-bandwidth workloads
5. **Maglev LB**: Consistent hashing for stable backend selection
6. **DSR mode**: Responses bypass load balancer, lower latency
7. **BGP control plane**: Dynamic route advertisement

## Implementation Steps

1. [ ] Audit current Cilium ConfigMap values
2. [ ] Enable `kubeProxyReplacement: true`
3. [ ] Configure `bpf.datapathMode: netkit`
4. [ ] Enable bandwidth manager with BBR
5. [ ] Enable IPv4 BIGTCP
6. [ ] Configure Maglev load balancing with DSR
7. [ ] Enable BGP control plane (if using BGP)
8. [ ] Apply incrementally and test after each change
9. [ ] Monitor Hubble metrics for improvements

## Sample Values

```yaml
kubeProxyReplacement: true
routingMode: native
autoDirectNodeRoutes: true

bpf:
  datapathMode: netkit
  masquerade: true
  tproxy: true

bandwidthManager:
  enabled: true
  bbr: true

ipv4:
  enabled: true
enableIPv4BIGTCP: true

loadBalancer:
  algorithm: maglev
  mode: dsr
  acceleration: native

bgpControlPlane:
  enabled: true

operator:
  replicas: 2
  prometheus:
    enabled: true

prometheus:
  enabled: true
```

## Risks & Considerations

- **kubeProxyReplacement**: Requires all nodes to have Cilium, no mixed environments
- **netkit**: Requires Linux kernel 6.8+ (check Talos version)
- **DSR mode**: May need firewall adjustments for asymmetric routing
- **BGP**: Requires compatible network infrastructure

## Resources

- [Cilium Performance Tuning](https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/operations/performance/tuning/)
- [BPF Datapath Modes](https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/network/concepts/routing/#datapath-modes)
- [Bandwidth Manager](https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/network/kubernetes/bandwidth-manager/)
- [Maglev Load Balancing](https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/network/lb-maglev/)

title: Deploy standalone Envoy Gateway
description:
Deploy a standalone Envoy Gateway for more customization than Cilium's built-in Gateway API implementation.

## Rationale

Cilium's built-in Gateway API is minimal. A standalone Envoy Gateway provides:
- More configuration options (compression, retries, keepalives)
- Better observability (Prometheus metrics)
- Advanced traffic policies (ClientTrafficPolicy, BackendTrafficPolicy)
- HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 support
- Fine-grained TLS configuration

## Reference Implementation

Based on: https://github.com/buroa/k8s-gitops/tree/main/kubernetes/apps/networking/envoy-gateway

## Architecture

```
                   Cilium LB IPAM
                        │
            ┌───────────┴───────────┐
            ▼                       ▼
    192.168.20.XX            192.168.20.YY
    envoy-external           envoy-internal
            │                       │
            ▼                       ▼
      HTTPRoutes               HTTPRoutes
    (public apps)           (internal apps)
```

## Key Features to Configure

### EnvoyProxy Resource
- 2 replicas for HA
- Resource limits (100m CPU, 2Gi memory)
- 180s drain timeout for graceful shutdown
- Prometheus metrics with Zstd compression
- Info-level logging

### GatewayClass
- Named `envoy` with controller `gateway.envoyproxy.io/gatewayclass-controller`
- References EnvoyProxy config via parametersRef

### Gateways (2)
- **envoy-external**: Public traffic via Cloudflare Tunnel
- **envoy-internal**: LAN + Tailscale traffic
- Both use `lbipam.cilium.io/ips` for static IP assignment
- HTTP (port 80) + HTTPS (port 443) listeners

### BackendTrafficPolicy
- Compression: Zstd, Brotli, Gzip
- Retries: 2 retries on connection reset
- TCP keepalive enabled

### ClientTrafficPolicy
- Client IP detection via X-Forwarded-For (from pod CIDR)
- TLS 1.2 minimum
- HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 support

### HTTPRoute (redirect)
- HTTP → HTTPS redirect (301)

## Implementation Steps

### Phase 1: Deploy Envoy Gateway Operator
1. [ ] Create `kubernetes/apps/network/envoy-gateway/` directory structure
2. [ ] Create `ks.yaml` with two Kustomizations (app + proxy)
3. [ ] Create `app/helmrelease.yaml` using `oci://docker.io/envoyproxy/gateway-helm`
4. [ ] Configure provider for Kubernetes + GatewayNamespace deployment

### Phase 2: Configure Proxy & Gateways
5. [ ] Create `proxy/envoy.yaml` with EnvoyProxy, GatewayClass, Gateways
6. [ ] Allocate IPs from Cilium LB IPAM pool
7. [ ] Create BackendTrafficPolicy for compression/retries
8. [ ] Create ClientTrafficPolicy for TLS/HTTP2/HTTP3
9. [ ] Create HTTP→HTTPS redirect HTTPRoute
10. [ ] Create `proxy/certificate.yaml` for TLS (reference existing cert or create new)

### Phase 3: Observability
11. [ ] Create `proxy/podmonitor.yaml` for Prometheus scraping
12. [ ] Verify metrics in Grafana

### Phase 4: Migration
13. [ ] Test with a single app (e.g., echo-server)
14. [ ] Migrate apps from Cilium Gateway to Envoy Gateway
15. [ ] Update existing HTTPRoutes to reference new gateways
16. [ ] Remove old Cilium gateways when migration complete

## Files to Create
```
kubernetes/apps/network/envoy-gateway/
├── ks.yaml
├── app/
│   ├── kustomization.yaml
│   └── helmrelease.yaml
└── proxy/
    ├── kustomization.yaml
    ├── envoy.yaml          # EnvoyProxy, GatewayClass, Gateways, Policies
    ├── certificate.yaml
    └── podmonitor.yaml
```

## Helm Chart
```yaml
repository: oci://docker.io/envoyproxy/gateway-helm
chart: gateway-helm
version: v1.3.x  # or latest stable
```

## Cilium Integration
- Uses `lbipam.cilium.io/ips` annotation for static LoadBalancer IPs
- Cilium continues to handle L3/L4 load balancing
- Envoy handles L7 (HTTP/HTTPS) processing

## Related Issues
- May interact with homelab-6ae (Gateway API migration) - could replace Cilium gateways entirely
- May interact with homelab-6rb (TinyCA) - certificates for internal gateway

## Resources
- [Envoy Gateway Docs](https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/)
- [Reference: buroa/k8s-gitops](https://github.com/buroa/k8s-gitops/tree/main/kubernetes/apps/networking/envoy-gateway)
- [Gateway API Spec](https://gateway-api.sigs.k8s.io/)

title: Set up noreply/SMTP relay with Maddy
description:
Deploy Maddy as an SMTP relay for sending outbound emails from cluster applications.

## Background

Currently no internal SMTP relay exists. Apps requiring email:
- **rxresume**: Has SMTP_URL commented out, uses `noreply@zebernst.dev`
- **Authentik** (future): Will need email for notifications, password resets
- **Grafana**: Alert notifications (currently likely disabled)
- **Other apps**: Various notification needs

## Maddy Overview

Maddy is a composable all-in-one mail server that can act as:
- SMTP relay (outbound only - our use case)
- Full mail server (not needed)

For our use case, configure as **outbound-only relay** that:
1. Accepts mail from cluster pods
2. Relays via upstream provider (e.g., Mailgun, SendGrid, or direct)
3. Signs with DKIM

## Implementation Steps

### Phase 1: DNS Setup
1. [ ] Decide on sending domain: `zebernst.dev` or subdomain like `mail.zebernst.dev`
2. [ ] Create SPF record: `v=spf1 include:_spf.mx.cloudflare.net ~all` (or relay provider)
3. [ ] Generate DKIM keys and add DNS record
4. [ ] Create DMARC record: `v=DMARC1; p=quarantine; rua=mailto:dmarc@zebernst.dev`

### Phase 2: Deploy Maddy
5. [ ] Create `network/maddy/` directory structure
6. [ ] Create HelmRelease or raw manifests for Maddy
7. [ ] Configure as outbound-only relay
8. [ ] Store DKIM private key in 1Password/external-secrets
9. [ ] Expose on internal ClusterIP (smtp.network.svc.cluster.local:25/587)

### Phase 3: Configuration
10. [ ] Configure upstream relay (direct, Mailgun, SendGrid, etc.)
11. [ ] Set up sender address restrictions (only `*@zebernst.dev`)
12. [ ] Configure TLS for STARTTLS support
13. [ ] Set up logging/monitoring

### Phase 4: Integrate Applications
14. [ ] **rxresume**: Uncomment and configure SMTP_URL
15. [ ] **Authentik**: Configure SMTP settings (when deployed)
16. [ ] **Grafana**: Configure SMTP for alert notifications
17. [ ] Document SMTP endpoint for future apps

## Maddy Configuration Example

```
# /etc/maddy/maddy.conf
smtp tcp://0.0.0.0:25 tcp://0.0.0.0:587 {
    limits {
        all rate 20 1s
    }

    dkim zebernst.dev {
        key_path /secrets/dkim.key
        selector mail
        domain zebernst.dev
    }

    deliver_to &remote_queue
}

target.queue remote_queue {
    target &outbound_delivery
}

target.smtp outbound_delivery {
    # Direct delivery or relay config
}
```

## Files to Create
- `kubernetes/apps/network/maddy/ks.yaml`
- `kubernetes/apps/network/maddy/app/helmrelease.yaml` (or deployment.yaml)
- `kubernetes/apps/network/maddy/app/configmap.yaml`
- `kubernetes/apps/network/maddy/app/externalsecret.yaml`
- `kubernetes/apps/network/maddy/app/kustomization.yaml`

## Service Endpoint
```
smtp.network.svc.cluster.local:25   # SMTP
smtp.network.svc.cluster.local:587  # Submission (STARTTLS)
```

## Related Issues
- homelab-nsp (Authentik) - needs email for notifications

## Resources
- [Maddy Docs](https://maddy.email/)
- [Maddy Docker Image](https://hub.docker.com/r/foxcpp/maddy)
- [Outbound-only setup guide](https://maddy.email/tutorials/smtp-relay/)

title: Migrate all ingress resources to Gateway API

Migrate all remaining Ingress resources to Gateway API HTTPRoutes.

title: Standardize Flux/Kustomize resource conventions
description:
Standardize differences amongst the Flux/Kustomize resources across the codebase for consistency and maintainability.

## Current State Analysis

**83 ks.yaml files** with the following inconsistencies:

### Schema Comments
- 69/83 have `yaml-language-server` schema comment
- 14 missing schema comments

### Namespace Aliases
- Most use `&ns` (87 occurrences)
- Some use `&namespace` (10 occurrences)
- Should standardize on `&ns`

### wait: Setting
- 72 have `wait: false`
- 28 have `wait: true`
- Need to determine correct policy per resource type

### interval: Values
- 90 use `30m`
- 12 use `1h`
- Should standardize (likely `30m` for most)

### retryInterval:
- 82/83 have `retryInterval`
- 1 missing

### prune: Setting
- 7 have `prune: false` (intentional for critical resources like flux-operator)
- Rest have `prune: true`

### Key Ordering
Varies across files. Proposed standard order:
```yaml
spec:
  targetNamespace: *ns
  commonMetadata:
    labels: ...
  dependsOn: ...
  path: ...
  components: ...    # if used
  postBuild: ...     # if used
  healthCheckExprs:  # if used
  prune: true
  sourceRef: ...
  wait: false
  interval: 30m
  retryInterval: 1m
  timeout: 5m
```

## Implementation Steps

### Phase 1: Define Standards
1. [ ] Document the canonical ks.yaml structure
2. [ ] Determine wait: policy (false for apps, true for operators/CRDs?)
3. [ ] Decide on interval (30m standard, 1h for slow-changing?)

### Phase 2: Automated Fixes
4. [ ] Add missing yaml-language-server schema comments
5. [ ] Standardize namespace alias `&namespace` → `&ns`
6. [ ] Add missing retryInterval fields
7. [ ] Normalize interval values

### Phase 3: Manual Review
8. [ ] Review key ordering in each file
9. [ ] Verify wait: values are intentional
10. [ ] Check prune: false is only on critical resources

### Phase 4: Tooling
11. [ ] Consider adding a linter/formatter (e.g., yamlfmt with custom config)
12. [ ] Add pre-commit hook to enforce standards

## Files Affected
- All 83 `kubernetes/apps/**/ks.yaml` files
- Potentially `kubernetes/apps/**/kustomization.yaml` files too

title: Priority Classes for Workload Scheduling
description:
Establish a tiered PriorityClass hierarchy so critical infrastructure survives resource pressure.

## Current State

**Existing files (uncommitted, need updating):**
- `priority-class/control-plane-critical.yaml` (1500) ✓
- `priority-class/network-critical.yaml` (1750) ✓
- `priority-class/security-critical.yaml` (1400) - to be removed
- `priority-class/external-facing.yaml` (200) - needs value change to 500
- `priority-class/media-high.yaml` (300) - rename to media-core

**Need to create:**
- `storage-critical.yaml` (2000)
- `database-critical.yaml` (1200)
- `internal-only.yaml` (100)
- `background.yaml` (50)
- `best-effort.yaml` (0)

**Already using built-in classes:**
- rook-ceph: `system-cluster-critical`
- generic-device-plugin: `system-node-critical`

## Final Hierarchy

| Class | Value | Workloads |
|-------|-------|-----------|
| storage-critical | 2000 | rook-ceph*, openebs, synology-csi, volsync, snapshot-controller |
| network-critical | 1750 | cloudflared, external-dns, ingress-nginx, tailscale-operator |
| control-plane-critical | 1500 | flux, cert-manager, external-secrets, prometheus-stack, grafana, loki, metrics-server |
| database-critical | 1200 | cloudnative-pg, dragonfly, mariadb |
| external-facing | 500 | plex, wizarr, overseerr, rxresume, gatus, kromgo |
| media-core | 300 | sonarr, radarr, lidarr, prowlarr, bazarr, qbittorrent, autobrr, tautulli, calibre-web, maintainerr, booklore |
| internal-only | 100 | glance, atuin, radicale, nocodb, mealie, paperless, homebox, hajimari, it-tools |
| background | 50 | recyclarr, unpackerr, cross-seed, omegabrr, flaresolverr, stash |
| best-effort | 0 | ollama, local-ai, steam, openbooks, minecraft |

*rook-ceph already uses system-cluster-critical

## Implementation Steps

### Phase 1: Create/Update PriorityClass files
1. [ ] Update `external-facing.yaml`: change value 200 → 500
2. [ ] Rename `media-high.yaml` → `media-core.yaml`
3. [ ] Delete `security-critical.yaml` (merged into control-plane)
4. [ ] Create `storage-critical.yaml` (2000)
5. [ ] Create `database-critical.yaml` (1200)
6. [ ] Create `internal-only.yaml` (100)
7. [ ] Create `background.yaml` (50)
8. [ ] Create `best-effort.yaml` (0)
9. [ ] Update `kube-system/kustomization.yaml` with new files

### Phase 2: Apply to workloads (by namespace)
10. [ ] kube-system: Apply to cilium, coredns, metrics-server, etc.
11. [ ] rook-ceph: Verify system-cluster-critical (already set)
12. [ ] openebs-system: Apply storage-critical
13. [ ] volsync-system: Apply storage-critical
14. [ ] network: Apply network-critical to cloudflared, external-dns, etc.
15. [ ] flux-system: Apply control-plane-critical
16. [ ] cert-manager: Apply control-plane-critical
17. [ ] external-secrets: Apply control-plane-critical
18. [ ] observability: Apply control-plane-critical
19. [ ] database: Apply database-critical
20. [ ] media: Apply external-facing (plex, wizarr, overseerr) and media-core (rest)
21. [ ] downloads: Apply media-core and background
22. [ ] self-hosted: Apply internal-only
23. [ ] ai: Apply best-effort
24. [ ] games: Apply best-effort

### Phase 3: Validation
25. [ ] Deploy changes and verify PriorityClasses exist
26. [ ] Verify pods have correct priorityClassName
27. [ ] Test preemption under resource pressure (optional)

## Files Changed
- `kubernetes/apps/kube-system/priority-class/*.yaml`
- `kubernetes/apps/kube-system/kustomization.yaml`
- HelmReleases across all namespaces (priorityClassName field)

title: Use Tuppr for Talos upgrades
description:
Adopt Tuppr for managing Talos Linux upgrades instead of manual talosctl commands. Set up Tuppr to handle rolling upgrades across the cluster nodes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tuppr deployed and configured for the cluster
- Rolling Talos upgrades automated via Tuppr
- Upgrade process respects node cordoning and PDB constraints

title: Migrate from Overseerr to Seerr
description:
Replace Overseerr with Seerr as the media request manager. Migrate any existing request data if possible, update ingress/HTTPRoutes, and reconfigure downstream integrations (Sonarr/Radarr).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Seerr deployed and accessible via existing request hostname
- Sonarr/Radarr integrations configured
- Existing request history migrated if possible
- Overseerr removed from cluster

title: Migrate from qbtools to tqm + qbrr
description:
Replace qbtools with tqm (torrent queue manager) and qbrr for qBittorrent management. Remove existing qbtools deployment and replace with the new tools.

## Acceptance Criteria
- tqm and qbrr deployed and managing qBittorrent
- qbtools removed from cluster
- Torrent management automation working (tag/category/cleanup)
notes: qui deployed as qBittorrent alternative UI. qbtools reannounce controller removed (qui handles reannouncing via autobrr). Remaining: deploy tqm + qbrr and remove qbtools entirely.

title: Deploy Notifier webhook service
description:
Deploy a Notifier webhook service for centralized notification routing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Notifier service deployed and healthy
- Webhook endpoints configured for notification sources
- Pushover/other notification targets connected

title: Deploy Agregarr for media request aggregation
description:
Deploy Agregarr for media request aggregation across multiple services.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Agregarr deployed and accessible
- Connected to media request sources
- Aggregated view of requests functional

title: Evaluate Volsync perfectra1n fork
description:
Evaluate whether to switch from standard Volsync to the perfectra1n fork used by reference repos.

## Current Setup (Your Repo)

Using standard Volsync from backube:

```yaml
# kubernetes/apps/volsync-system/volsync/
# Standard Volsync deployment
```

## Reference Setup (onedr0p)

Uses **perfectra1n fork** of Volsync:

```yaml
image:
  repository: ghcr.io/perfectra1n/volsync
  tag: 0.17.6
```

Features of the fork:
- **Multiple backup tools**: Kopia, Rclone, Restic, Rsync, Rsync-TLS, Syncthing
- **Enhanced security**: Non-root UID 1000, read-only considerations
- **CRD Management**: Automatic CRD updates
- **Bug fixes**: Community patches not yet in upstream

## Investigation Needed

1. [ ] Research what the perfectra1n fork adds over upstream
2. [ ] Check GitHub for fork differences/commits
3. [ ] Evaluate if the additional features are useful
4. [ ] Test compatibility with existing ReplicationSources
5. [ ] Decide: migrate or stay with upstream

## Questions to Answer

- What specific features does the fork add?
- Is it actively maintained?
- Are there breaking changes from upstream?
- Will existing backup configurations continue to work?

## Resources

- [perfectra1n/volsync](https://github.com/perfectra1n/volsync)
- [backube/volsync (upstream)](https://github.com/backube/volsync)
- Compare commits between fork and upstream

title: Deploy Qui as qBittorrent alternative UI
description:
Deploy Qui as an alternative web UI for qBittorrent.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Qui deployed and connected to qBittorrent API
- Accessible via internal HTTPRoute
- Functional torrent management through Qui interface
close_reason: Deployed qui: HelmRelease with app-template, ExternalSecret, Volsync PVC, tailscale + internal HTTPRoute at qui.zebernst.dev, metrics on port 9074 with ServiceMonitor, NFS mount for orphan scan/dir-scan/cross-seed

title: Deploy Actions Runner Controller for self-hosted CI/CD
description:
Deploy Actions Runner Controller for self-hosted GitHub Actions CI/CD runners in the cluster.

## Acceptance Criteria
- ARC deployed with runner scale sets
- Runners registering with GitHub org/repos
- Jobs executing successfully on self-hosted runners
- Autoscaling configured based on job queue

title: Evaluate Tuppr vs system-upgrade-controller
description:
Evaluate whether to migrate from system-upgrade-controller to Tuppr for Talos/Kubernetes upgrades.

## Current Setup (Your Repo)

```
kubernetes/apps/system-upgrade/
├── system-upgrade-controller/
└── versions.env
```

Using standard **system-upgrade-controller** for coordinated node upgrades.

## Reference Setup (buroa & onedr0p)

Both repos use **Tuppr**:

```
kubernetes/apps/system-upgrade/
├── tuppr/
│   ├── app/
│   └── upgrades/
```

Tuppr appears to be a wrapper/alternative that provides:
- App deployment configuration
- Separate upgrades configuration
- Potentially more features for Talos-specific upgrades

## Investigation Needed

1. [ ] Research what Tuppr actually is and how it differs
2. [ ] Check if Tuppr is a community project or custom solution
3. [ ] Compare feature sets:
   - Rolling upgrade support
   - Drain/cordon behavior
   - Talos-specific integration
   - Plan approval workflow
4. [ ] Evaluate migration complexity
5. [ ] Decide: migrate or stay with system-upgrade-controller

## Questions to Answer

- Is Tuppr a Helm chart or custom manifests?
- Does it provide better Talos Linux integration?
- What's the upgrade coordination model?
- Is there a web UI or CLI for managing upgrades?

## Resources

- [system-upgrade-controller](https://github.com/rancher/system-upgrade-controller)
- Check buroa/onedr0p repos for Tuppr source/docs

title: Migrate from Promtail to Fluent Bit
description:
Migrate log collection from Promtail to Fluent Bit for better performance and flexibility.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Fluent Bit deployed as DaemonSet
- All node and pod logs collected
- Logs forwarded to existing log storage backend
- Promtail removed from cluster
close_reason: Fluent Bit deployed, sending logs to Victoria Logs

title: Migrate from Loki to Victoria Logs
description:
Migrate log storage from Loki to Victoria Logs for improved performance and resource usage.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Victoria Logs deployed with persistent storage
- Log ingestion pipeline configured (from Fluent Bit or Promtail)
- Grafana datasource configured for Victoria Logs
- Historical logs migrated or retention policy accepted
- Loki removed from cluster
close_reason: Victoria Logs deployed with 20Gi persistent storage and Grafana datasource

title: Evaluate migration from Taskfile to Justfile
description:
Evaluate and potentially migrate from Taskfile (YAML) to Justfile (Make-like DSL) for task automation.

## Background

onedr0p/home-ops has migrated to Justfile while buroa/k8s-gitops still uses Taskfile. This is a low-priority exploration to determine if migration is worthwhile.

## Current Setup

- `Taskfile.yaml` at root
- Modules in `.taskfiles/` (bootstrap, kubernetes, synology, talos, volsync)
- ~25 tasks total
- Uses: preconditions, prompts, variable interpolation, dotenv

## Comparison Summary

| Feature | Taskfile (Current) | Just |
|---------|-------------------|------|
| Syntax | YAML | Make-like DSL |
| Parameters | `VAR=value` | Positional args |
| Preconditions | Built-in | Manual shell checks |
| Prompts | Built-in | Requires `gum` |
| Schema validation | Yes | No |
| Multi-language recipes | Limited | Excellent |
| Startup time | ~10-20ms | ~2-3ms |

## Benefits of Just

1. Cleaner CLI: `just deploy prod` vs `task deploy ENV=prod`
2. Multi-language recipes (Python, Node, etc.)
3. Simpler syntax for shell-heavy tasks
4. Built-in shell completions
5. Faster startup (Rust vs Go)

## Drawbacks of Just

1. Loss of declarative `preconditions:`
2. Loss of built-in `prompt:` (need `gum`)
3. No JSON Schema for IDE validation
4. No checksum-based task skipping
5. Learning curve for team
6. Migration effort (~25 tasks)

## Implementation Steps (if proceeding)

### Phase 1: Evaluation
1. [ ] Install `just` via mise
2. [ ] Create proof-of-concept `.justfile` with 2-3 tasks
3. [ ] Test ergonomics and identify pain points
4. [ ] Decide go/no-go

### Phase 2: Migration (if approved)
5. [ ] Create root `.justfile` with shell config and helpers
6. [ ] Create `bootstrap/mod.just`
7. [ ] Create `kubernetes/mod.just`
8. [ ] Create `talos/mod.just`
9. [ ] Create `volsync/mod.just` (if needed)
10. [ ] Add `gum` to mise for interactive prompts
11. [ ] Test all recipes
12. [ ] Remove old `.taskfiles/` and `Taskfile.yaml`
13. [ ] Update CLAUDE.md documentation

## Reference Implementation

onedr0p/home-ops structure:
```
.justfile              # Root with imports
bootstrap/mod.just     # Bootstrap recipes
kubernetes/mod.just    # K8s recipes  
talos/mod.just         # Talos recipes
```

Key patterns:
- `[private]` attribute for helper recipes
- `gum log` for structured logging
- `gum confirm` for prompts

## Resources

- [Just Manual](https://just.systems/man/en/)
- [onedr0p/home-ops .justfile](https://github.com/onedr0p/home-ops/blob/main/.justfile)
- [Taskfile vs Justfile comparison](https://nguyenhuythanh.com/posts/taskfile-vs-justfile/)
